ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general wonton entry; no specific entry for pork-vegetable wontons in peanut sesame sauce. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for wontons in peanut sesame sauce; A0CKP (wonton-based dishes) is the closest defensible anchor. Sauce variant not separately coded.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code exists for this dish. SNOMED CT does not enumerate individual prepared Chinese dishes at this level of specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of vegetables and pork wontons in peanut sesame sauce is approximately 250 g (roughly 8–10 wontons with sauce), as commonly served in a Singaporean hawker or restaurant setting.

nutrient_source.title: Vegetables & Pork Wantons In Peanut Sesame Sauce — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 麻酱菜肉馄饨 Vegetables & Pork Wantons In Peanut Sesame Sauce (cr_id G00010058). All per-100g values are taken as reported. Carbohydrate is recorded as 0.0 g/100g in the HPB source; this is noted as a data anomaly given the presence of wonton wrappers — see notes. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several micronutrients are not reported in the HPB entry and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on boiled wheat-based dumplings / wontons
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for pork-vegetable wontons in peanut sesame sauce. GI estimated at approximately 45 (low) based on the closest analogous food: boiled wheat-based dumplings and wontons, which have a GI of approximately 40–55 in the University of Sydney GI Database. The peanut-sesame sauce contributes fat and protein that further attenuate glycaemic response. Glycemic load per serving is calculated as approximately 0.0 g because the HPB source records carbohydrate as 0.0 g/100g; however, this is a data anomaly — actual GL would be higher if wonton wrapper carbohydrates were fully captured. GL is reported as 0.0 per the source data with this caveat noted.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1050 mg/100g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100g upper threshold
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; sodium at 1050 mg/100g represents a very high cardiovascular load, and requesting sauce on the side or consuming a half-portion (≤125 g) is strongly advised to keep sodium intake within safe limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Wonton wrappers contribute refined carbohydrates not fully captured in the HPB record; peanut-sesame sauce may contain added sugar
type2_diabetes.guidance: While the HPB record shows 0 g carbohydrate/100g, wonton wrappers are made from refined wheat flour and will raise postprandial glucose; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 150–200 g, verify sauce ingredients for added sugar, and pair with non-starchy vegetables to moderate glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium 1050 mg/100g and cholesterol 89 mg/100g; pork filling and peanut-sesame sauce contribute saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish is high in sodium and cholesterol, both of which are adverse for cardiovascular health; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to small portions (≤100 g), request reduced-sodium sauce, and avoid frequent intake given the combined sodium and saturated fat burden from pork and peanut sauce.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium 1050 mg/100g exceeds RA threshold of 400 mg/100g; pork contributes arachidonic acid amplifying inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish due to very high sodium content worsening tissue swelling and pork-derived arachidonic acid amplifying inflammatory cytokine activity; substituting pork with tofu or chicken and requesting reduced-sodium sauce are recommended if consumed.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vegetables and Pork Wontons in Peanut Sesame Sauce (麻酱菜肉馄饨) is a Chinese-origin dish popular in Singapore's hawker and restaurant scene. Wontons are thin wheat-flour dumplings filled with a mixture of minced pork and finely chopped vegetables, then boiled until tender and tossed in a rich, bold peanut-sesame sauce. The sauce is typically made from peanut butter or ground peanuts, sesame paste (zhīma jiàng), sesame oil, soy sauce, vinegar, and chilli, delivering a complex flavour profile that is simultaneously nutty, savoury, slightly spicy, and aromatic. The texture contrast between the silky wonton skin and the dense, flavourful filling, coated in the thick sauce, makes this a satisfying and indulgent dish. Nutritionally, the HPB Singapore database records 171 kcal, 19.1 g protein, and 10.5 g fat per 100 g, with sodium at a very high 1050 mg/100g — a significant clinical concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease. Carbohydrate is recorded as 0.0 g/100g in the source, which is a known data anomaly as wonton wrappers contribute refined wheat carbohydrates; consumers with Type 2 Diabetes should account for this. The dish is not suitable for halal or kosher diets due to pork content, and contains major allergens including gluten, peanuts, sesame, and soy. It is commonly served as a main course at lunch or dinner.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010058, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. The carbohydrate anomaly (0.0 g/100g) is documented and does not invalidate the record — it reflects the HPB source as-is. All other values are taken from the approved source with no estimation required for core macros.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is recorded as 0.0 g/100g per the HPB Singapore Foodo source. This is a confirmed data anomaly: wonton wrappers are made from refined wheat flour and would be expected to contribute approximately 20–30 g carbohydrate per 100 g of the finished dish. The 0.0 g value is preserved as reported by the approved source (HPB) and is flagged here for downstream review. Sugar_g is 0.0 g/100g per HPB source; peanut-sesame sauce may contain added sugar not captured — confirmed zero per HPB source data but flagged for recipe-level verification. Dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 g/100g per HPB source; confirmed zero per HPB. Saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry as of 2026-04-16. Vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this dish as of 2026-04-16. GI is an analogous estimate based on boiled wheat-based wontons/dumplings from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish exists in approved sources. GL per serving is reported as 0.0 due to the carbohydrate anomaly in the source data; actual GL would be materially higher if carbohydrate content were correctly captured.
